calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion And Motion To Augment The Record
Off Calendar Jan-14-2025 Off Calendar

DEMURRER TO PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION
Off Calendar May-26-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Attorney DONALD H. MEDEARIS, Esq. of Bay Area Legal Aid (Tel: 415-354-6331) appearing via CourtCall for the Petitioner;
Deputy City Attorney NEHA GUPTA, Esq. of the San Francisco City Attorney's Office (415-554-4665) appearing via CourtCall for the Respondents.

HEARING RE: RESPONDENTS CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, HUMAN SERVICES AGENCY OF THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, BOARD OF SUPERVISORS OF THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, AND HUMAN SERVICES COMMISSION OF THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S DEMURRER TO PETITIONER'S SECOND CAUSE OF ACTION FOR WRIT OF ORDINARY MANDAMUS IN FIRST AMENDED PETITION.

Matter argued and submitted by counsel for both sides. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows:

Respondents' demurrer to petitioner's second cause of action in the amended petition for ordinary mandamus per Code of Civil Procedure 1085 is sustained without leave to amend.

Respondents imposed a 60-day suspension of petitioner's County Adult Assistance Programs ("CAAP") benefits as a sanction because they concluded that petitioner underreported his income for a second time without cause. Petitioner's second cause of action alleges that CAAP Eligibility Manual 94-21 is contrary to Welfare & Institution Code 17001.5(a) and San Francisco Administrative Code 20.7-44 because respondents have a practice of discontinuing benefits without determining whether the recipient made a false statement or intentionally failed to report facts.

CAAP Eligibility Manual 94-21 provides that: "all second and subsequent discoveries of a client's not reporting income?result in a negative action, whether or not fraud can be established, even when no contradictory evidence exists." Read in isolation, petitioner's assertion appears to have merit. Nevertheless, the entire municipal code and the decision itself show that respondents' scheme complies with the good cause or fraud determination requirements of the adduced provisions.

The court must examine the legislative framework as a whole. (See People v. Murphy (2001) 25 Cal.4th 136, 142 ["We must harmonize the various parts of a statutory enactment . . . by considering the particular clause or section in the context of the statutory framework as a whole" (internal quotations omitted)].) The legislative provisions show that when respondents learned of petitioner's second failure to report income, it did not immediately sanction him. Rather, respondents served a notice of proposed action and gave petitioner a hearing to challenge the proposed sanction and to show good cause. (S.F. Admin. Code 20.7-45 [hearing].) If petitioner's interpretation of CAAP Eligibility Manual 94-21 were correct, then respondents would have immediately sanctioned him rather than provide him with a hearing.

The decision itself, which is attached to the first amended petition, reveals the fallacy of plaintiff's second cause of action. A major portion of the decision discusses the hearing officer's attempt to verify and/or seek information regarding petitioner's alleged delusional disorder. The purpose of that inquiry was to determine whether there was good cause for petitioner's failure to report his income. In other words, the hearing officer sought to determine whether petitioner's claimed disorder caused the failure to report the income and petitioner did not engage in a willful failure to report income. The decision is thus completely at odds with petitioner's position. In sum, given the legislative scheme as applied and the basis for the decision, petitioner's second cause of action fails as a matter of law.

The Court will enter the proposed order submitted by Respondents' counsel.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)
